name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَهَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ مُوسَىٰٓ (٩)
```

```english
Did the story of Moses come to you, (9)
```

```arabic
إِذْ رَءَا نَارًا فَقَالَ لِأَهْلِهِ ٱمْكُثُوٓا۟ إِنِّىٓ ءَانَسْتُ نَارًا لَّعَلِّىٓ ءَاتِيكُم مِّنْهَا بِقَبَسٍ أَوْ أَجِدُ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ هُدًى (١٠)
```

```english
when he sighted a fire, and said to his family, ‘Wait! Indeed, I descry a fire! Maybe I will bring you a brand from it, or find some guidance at the fire.’ (10)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّآ أَتَىٰهَا نُودِىَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ (١١)
```

```english
So when he came to it, he was called, ‘O Moses! (11)
```

```arabic
إِنِّىٓ أَنَا۠ رَبُّكَ فَٱخْلَعْ نَعْلَيْكَ ۖ إِنَّكَ بِٱلْوَادِ ٱلْمُقَدَّسِ طُوًى (١٢)
```

```english
Indeed I am your Lord! So take off your sandals. You are indeed in the sacred valley of Tuwa. (12)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَا ٱخْتَرْتُكَ فَٱسْتَمِعْ لِمَا يُوحَىٰٓ (١٣)
```

```english
I have chosen you; so listen to what is revealed. (13)
```

```arabic
إِنَّنِىٓ أَنَا ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠ فَٱعْبُدْنِى وَأَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ لِذِكْرِىٓ (١٤)
```

```english
Indeed I am Allah—there is no god except Me. So worship Me, and maintain the prayer for My remembrance. (14)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ ءَاتِيَةٌ أَكَادُ أُخْفِيهَا لِتُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا تَسْعَىٰ (١٥)
```

```english
Indeed the Hour is bound to come: I will have it hidden, so that every soul may be rewarded for its endeavour. (15)
```

```arabic
فَلَا يَصُدَّنَّكَ عَنْهَا مَن لَّا يُؤْمِنُ بِهَا وَٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ فَتَرْدَىٰ (١٦)
```

```english
So do not let yourself be distracted from it by those who do not believe in it and who follow their desires, or you will perish.’ (16)
```

```arabic
وَمَا تِلْكَ بِيَمِينِكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ (١٧)
```

```english
‘Moses, what is that in your right hand?’ (17)
```

```arabic
قَالَ هِىَ عَصَاىَ أَتَوَكَّؤُا۟ عَلَيْهَا وَأَهُشُّ بِهَا عَلَىٰ غَنَمِى وَلِىَ فِيهَا مَـَٔارِبُ أُخْرَىٰ (١٨)
```

```english
He said, ‘It is my staff. I lean on it, and with it I beat down leaves for my sheep; and I have other uses for it.’ (18)
```

```arabic
قَالَ أَلْقِهَا يَـٰمُوسَىٰ (١٩)
```

```english
He said, ‘Moses, throw it down.’ (19)
```

```arabic
فَأَلْقَىٰهَا فَإِذَا هِىَ حَيَّةٌ تَسْعَىٰ (٢٠)
```

```english
So he threw it down, and behold, it was a snake, moving swiftly. (20)
```

```arabic
قَالَ خُذْهَا وَلَا تَخَفْ ۖ سَنُعِيدُهَا سِيرَتَهَا ٱلْأُولَىٰ (٢١)
```

```english
He said, ‘Take hold of it, and do not fear. We will restore it to its former state. (21)
```

```arabic
وَٱضْمُمْ يَدَكَ إِلَىٰ جَنَاحِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضَآءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوٓءٍ ءَايَةً أُخْرَىٰ (٢٢)
```

```english
Now clasp your hand to your armpit: it will emerge white, without any harm—[this is yet] another sign, (22)
```

```arabic
لِنُرِيَكَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا ٱلْكُبْرَى (٢٣)
```

```english
that We may show you some of Our great signs. (23)
```

```arabic
ٱذْهَبْ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُۥ طَغَىٰ (٢٤)
```

```english
Go to Pharaoh. He has indeed rebelled.’ (24)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱشْرَحْ لِى صَدْرِى (٢٥)
```

```english
He said, ‘My Lord! Open my breast for me. (25)
```

```arabic
وَيَسِّرْ لِىٓ أَمْرِى (٢٦)
```

```english
Make my task easy for me. (26)
```

```arabic
وَٱحْلُلْ عُقْدَةً مِّن لِّسَانِى (٢٧)
```

```english
Remove the hitch from my tongue, (27)
```

```arabic
يَفْقَهُوا۟ قَوْلِى (٢٨)
```

```english
[so that] they may understand my speech. (28)
```

```arabic
وَٱجْعَل لِّى وَزِيرًا مِّنْ أَهْلِى (٢٩)
```

```english
Appoint for me a minister from my family, (29)
```

```arabic
هَـٰرُونَ أَخِى (٣٠)
```

```english
Aaron, my brother. (30)
```

```arabic
ٱشْدُدْ بِهِۦٓ أَزْرِى (٣١)
```

```english
Strengthen my back through him, (31)
```

```arabic
وَأَشْرِكْهُ فِىٓ أَمْرِى (٣٢)
```

```english
and make him my associate in my task, (32)
```

```arabic
كَىْ نُسَبِّحَكَ كَثِيرًا (٣٣)
```

```english
so that we may glorify You greatly, (33)
```

```arabic
وَنَذْكُرَكَ كَثِيرًا (٣٤)
```

```english
and remember You greatly. (34)
```

```arabic
إِنَّكَ كُنتَ بِنَا بَصِيرًا (٣٥)
```

```english
Indeed You see us best.’ (35)
```

```arabic
قَالَ قَدْ أُوتِيتَ سُؤْلَكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ (٣٦)
```

```english
He said, ‘Moses, your request has been granted! (36)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلَيْكَ مَرَّةً أُخْرَىٰٓ (٣٧)
```

```english
Certainly, We have done you a favour another time, (37)
```

```arabic
إِذْ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّكَ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ (٣٨)
```

```english
when We revealed to your mother whatever was to be revealed: (38)
```

```arabic
أَنِ ٱقْذِفِيهِ فِى ٱلتَّابُوتِ فَٱقْذِفِيهِ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ فَلْيُلْقِهِ ٱلْيَمُّ بِٱلسَّاحِلِ يَأْخُذْهُ عَدُوٌّ لِّى وَعَدُوٌّ لَّهُۥ ۚ وَأَلْقَيْتُ عَلَيْكَ مَحَبَّةً مِّنِّى وَلِتُصْنَعَ عَلَىٰ عَيْنِىٓ (٣٩)
```

```english
“Put him in the casket, and cast it into the river. Then the river will cast it on the bank, and he shall be picked up by an enemy of Mine and an enemy of his.” And I made you endearing, and that you might be reared under My [watchful] eyes. (39)
```

```arabic
إِذْ تَمْشِىٓ أُخْتُكَ فَتَقُولُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ مَن يَكْفُلُهُۥ ۖ فَرَجَعْنَـٰكَ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّكَ كَىْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُهَا وَلَا تَحْزَنَ ۚ وَقَتَلْتَ نَفْسًا فَنَجَّيْنَـٰكَ مِنَ ٱلْغَمِّ وَفَتَنَّـٰكَ فُتُونًا ۚ فَلَبِثْتَ سِنِينَ فِىٓ أَهْلِ مَدْيَنَ ثُمَّ جِئْتَ عَلَىٰ قَدَرٍ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ (٤٠)
```

```english
When your sister walked up [to Pharaoh’s palace] saying, “Shall I show you someone who will take care of him?” Then We restored you to your mother, that she might not grieve and be comforted. Then you slew a soul, whereupon We delivered you from anguish, and We tried you with various ordeals. Then you stayed for several years among the people of Midian. Then you turned up as ordained, O Moses! (40)
```

```arabic
وَٱصْطَنَعْتُكَ لِنَفْسِى (٤١)
```

```english
And I chose you for Myself.’ (41)
```

```arabic
ٱذْهَبْ أَنتَ وَأَخُوكَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِى وَلَا تَنِيَا فِى ذِكْرِى (٤٢)
```

```english
‘Go ahead, you and your brother, with My signs and do not flag in My remembrance. (42)
```

```arabic
ٱذْهَبَآ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُۥ طَغَىٰ (٤٣)
```

```english
Both of you go to Pharaoh, for he has indeed rebelled. (43)
```

```arabic
فَقُولَا لَهُۥ قَوْلًا لَّيِّنًا لَّعَلَّهُۥ يَتَذَكَّرُ أَوْ يَخْشَىٰ (٤٤)
```

```english
Speak to him in a soft manner; maybe he will take admonition or fear.’ (44)
```

```arabic
قَالَا رَبَّنَآ إِنَّنَا نَخَافُ أَن يَفْرُطَ عَلَيْنَآ أَوْ أَن يَطْغَىٰ (٤٥)
```

```english
They said, ‘Our Lord! We are indeed afraid that he will forestall us or will exceed all bounds.’ (45)
```

```arabic
قَالَ لَا تَخَافَآ ۖ إِنَّنِى مَعَكُمَآ أَسْمَعُ وَأَرَىٰ (٤٦)
```

```english
He said, ‘Do not be afraid, for I will be with the two of you, hearing and seeing [whatever happens]. (46)
```

```arabic
فَأْتِيَاهُ فَقُولَآ إِنَّا رَسُولَا رَبِّكَ فَأَرْسِلْ مَعَنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَلَا تُعَذِّبْهُمْ ۖ قَدْ جِئْنَـٰكَ بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۖ وَٱلسَّلَـٰمُ عَلَىٰ مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ (٤٧)
```

```english
So approach him and say, ‘‘We are the apostles of your Lord. Let the Children of Israel go with us, and do not torture them! We certainly bring you a sign from your Lord, and may peace be upon him who follows guidance! (47)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا قَدْ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْنَآ أَنَّ ٱلْعَذَابَ عَلَىٰ مَن كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ (٤٨)
```

```english
Indeed it has been revealed to us that punishment shall befall those who impugn us and turn their backs [on us].’’ ’ (48)
```

## (Commentary)

This marks the beginning of the story of Moses (peace be upon him). Four chapters of his story are mentioned in this Surah: First, the selection of Moses for the messengership at Mount *Tur* in the valley of *Tuwa*, and the command for him to call Pharaoh. Second, his call—joined by his brother—to Pharaoh toward monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*, the demand to send the Children of Israel with him, the establishment of proof, and the granting of miracles. Third, his departure with the Children of Israel from Egypt, the pursuit by Pharaoh, Pharaoh's drowning, and the salvation of the Children of Israel. Fourth, the worship of the calf by the Children of Israel, the outcome of their affair, and the matter of the *Samiri* and his calf. The verses we have cited address the first chapter of these events.

The connection of this story to what preceded it is that it serves as a reminder (تذكرة) of monotheism and a warning (وعيد) of punishment. The story begins with the revelation of monotheism and ends with the saying of Moses: **إِنَّما إِلهُكُمُ اللهُ الَّذِي لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** — "Your god is only Allah; there is no god except Him" (20:98), and mentions the destruction of Pharaoh and the expulsion of the *Samiri*. The preceding verses began by stating that the Quran, which contains the true call, is a reminder for those who fear, and concluded with His saying: **اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى** — "Allah—there is no god except Him. To Him belong the Best Names" (20:8).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهَلْ أَتاكَ حَدِيثُ مُوسى** — "Has the report of Moses reached you?" (20:9). The interrogation here is for affirmation (التقرير), and "report" (الحديث) refers to the story.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِذْ رَأى ناراً فَقالَ لِأَهْلِهِ امْكُثُوا إِنِّي آنَسْتُ ناراً** — "When he sighted a fire, and said to his family, ‘Wait! Indeed, I descry a fire...’" (20:10) until the end of the verse. "Waiting" (المكث) means tarrying, and "descrying" (الإيناس) is to perceive a thing or find it; it is derived from *uns* (familiarity), the opposite of *nufur* (aversion). Thus, it is said that it means perceiving something that brings comfort, which implies a strong perception. A "brand" (القبس) is a flame taken from the tip of a stick or similar object. "Guidance" (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — is a verbal noun used in the sense of an active participle, or it is a genitive construction where a word is omitted, meaning "one possessing guidance." In any case, it refers to someone who provides guidance.

The context of the verse and those following it testifies that this occurred during his return from Midian to Egypt with his family. They were near the valley of *Tuwa* at Mount Sinai on a dark, wintry night and had lost their way. When he saw a fire, he decided to go toward it; if he found someone there, he would ask for directions, otherwise, he would take a brand from the fire so they could kindle a fire to warm themselves.

In His saying, "and said to his family, ‘Wait!’" (فَقالَ لِأَهْلِهِ امْكُثُوا), there is an indication, or rather a proof, that there were others with his family. Similarly, in His saying, "Indeed, I descry a fire" (إِنِّي آنَسْتُ ناراً), along with the emphasis and the use of the term "descry" (الإيناس), there is proof that he alone saw it, while the others with him did not. This is further supported by the initial phrase: "When he sighted a fire" (إِذْ رَأى ناراً). Likewise, His saying, "perhaps I may bring you..." (لَعَلِّي آتِيكُمْ) indicates an omission in the speech, the implied meaning being: "Wait so that I may go to it, perhaps I may bring you a brand from it or find at the fire a guide by whose guidance we may be led."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلَمَّا أَتاها نُودِيَ يا مُوسى إِنِّي أَنَا رَبُّكَ** — "When he came to it, he was called, ‘O Moses! Indeed I am your Lord...’" (20:11-12) until His saying, "**طُوىً**" (Tuwa). *Tuwa* is the name of a valley at the Mount, which Allah, the Glorified, called "the holy valley" (الواد المقدس). This naming and description are the evidence that the command to remove his sandals was out of respect for the valley, so it would not be trodden upon with sandals. Furthermore, the fact that the removal of the sandals is branched (تفريع) from His saying, "Indeed I am your Lord," indicates that the holiness of the valley is due to it being a sanctuary of proximity and a place of presence and intimate discourse (المناجاة). Thus, the meaning of the verse resolves to something like: "He was called: O Moses, here I am, your Lord, and you are in My presence; the valley has become holy by this, so adhere to the requirements of etiquette and remove your sandals."

In this manner, certain places and times are sanctified, such as the Noble Kaaba, the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام), and other mosques and respected shrines (المشاهد) in Islam, as well as the Eids and blessed days. This holiness and nobility are acquired through association with a noble event that occurred therein or a sacred rite (نسك) and worship (عبادة) — *the act of submission and devotion to Allah* — legislated within them; otherwise, there is no inherent superiority between parts of space or parts of time.

When Moses (peace be upon him) heard the saying of the Exalted, "O Moses! Indeed I am your Lord," he understood with certain knowledge (فهم يقين) that the One speaking to him was his Lord and that the speech was His speech. This is because it was a revelation (وحي) from Him, the Exalted. Allah has explicitly stated: **وَما كانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُكَلِّمَهُ اللهُ إِلَّا وَحْياً أَوْ مِنْ وَراءِ حِجابٍ أَوْ يُرْسِلَ رَسُولاً فَيُوحِيَ بِإِذْنِهِ ما يَشاءُ** — "It is not for any human that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a veil, or by sending a messenger who reveals by His leave whatever He wishes" (42:51). This indicates that there is no intermediary between Him and the one He speaks to—neither a veil nor a messenger—when the speaking is by revelation (وحي). Since there was no assumed intermediary, the recipient of the revelation found no speaker for himself, nor could he imagine any, except Allah, and he found the speech to be none other than His speech. Had there been a possibility that the speaker was someone else or the speech belonged to another, it would not be a "speaking" where nothing exists between the human and his Lord.

This is the state of the Prophet and the Messenger at the very beginning of the revelation of prophethood (النبوة) and messengership (الرسالة); no doubt flickers within him, nor does any uncertainty cross his mind that the One revealing to him is Allah, the Glorified, without needing to employ discursive thought, seek evidence, or establish a proof. Had he required any of that, it would be an acquisition through the theoretical faculty (القوة النظرية), not a reception from the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — without an intermediary.

If you say: His saying, the Exalted, in the story in another place: **وَنادَيْناهُ مِنْ جانِبِ الطُّورِ الْأَيْمَنِ وَقَرَّبْناهُ نَجِيًّا** — "We called him from the right side of the Mount and drew him near for confidential discourse" (19:52), and His saying in another place: **مِنْ شاطِئِ الْوادِ الْأَيْمَنِ فِي الْبُقْعَةِ الْمُبارَكَةِ مِنَ الشَّجَرَةِ** — "...from the right bank of the valley in the blessed spot, from the tree" (28:30), establishes a "veil" (الحجاب) in His speaking to him (peace be upon him).

I say: Yes, but the existence of a veil or a messenger in the station of speaking does not negate the realization of speaking by revelation (وحي). For revelation, like all acts of the Exalted, is not devoid of an intermediary. The matter depends on the attention of the addressee who receives the speech. If he attends to the intermediary carrying the speech and is veiled by it from Him, the Exalted, then the speech is a "messengership" (رسالة) sent to him via an angel, for example, and a revelation from the angel. But if his attention is directed toward Him, the Exalted, then it is a revelation from Him, even if an intermediary exists to which he does not attend. Evidence for what we have mentioned is His saying in the following verse, addressing Moses: **فَاسْتَمِعْ لِما يُوحى** — "so listen to what is revealed" (20:13), wherein He called it "revelation" (وحي), despite having established the "veil" (الحجاب) in His other speech regarding it.

In short, His saying: "Indeed I am your Lord, so remove your sandals..." is an alerting of Moses to the fact that the situation is one of Presence and the station is one of direct address (المشافهة), and that He has secluded him and singled him out with increased care. For this reason, it was said: "Indeed I am your Lord," and He did not say: "I am Allah" or "I am the Lord of all the worlds." Also, for this reason, His second saying: "Indeed I am Allah" (إِنَّنِي أَنَا اللهُ) did not constitute a repetition, because the first was to clear the station of others for the delivery of revelation, and the second was part of the revelation itself.

In His saying: "he was called" (نُودِيَ), where the mention of the agent is omitted—He did not say: "We called him" or "Allah called him"—there is a subtlety beyond measure. It also hints that the appearance of this sign (الآية) to Moses occurred by way of surprise.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَنَا اخْتَرْتُكَ فَاسْتَمِعْ لِما يُوحى** — "I have chosen you, so listen to what is revealed" (20:13). "Selection" (الاختيار) is derived from *khayr* (good). Its reality is that the agent's affair fluctuates between several actions, and he must prefer one of them to perform; thus, he distinguishes which is the best (خيرها) and then acts upon its being better than others. His acting upon its being better than others is "selection." Therefore, selection is always for an end (غاية) which is the agent's purpose for his action.

Thus, His selection of Moses was for a divine end, which is the granting of prophethood and messengership. This is evidenced by His saying, as a branch of the selection: "so listen to what is revealed." The divine will (المشية الإلهية) was attached to sending a human to bear prophethood and messengership, and Moses was, in the knowledge of the Exalted, better than others and more fit for this purpose; so He chose him (peace be upon him).

His saying: "I have chosen you" (وَأَنَا اخْتَرْتُكَ), according to what the context provides, is of the nature of issuing the command for his prophethood and messengership; it is a performative (إنشاء), not an informative (إخبار) statement. Had it been informative, it would have been said: "And I have [already] chosen you." But it is the performative enactment of selection for prophethood and messengership through this very word. Then, once the selection was realized through His performative act, He branched from it the command to listen to the revelation containing his prophethood and messengership, saying: "so listen to what is revealed." Listening to what is revealed means paying close attention to it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّنِي أَنَا اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاعْبُدْنِي وَأَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِذِكْرِي** — "Indeed I am Allah—there is no god except Me. So worship Me, and maintain the prayer for My remembrance" (20:14). This is the revelation he (peace be upon him) was commanded to listen to, spanning eleven verses that encompass both prophethood and messengership. As for prophethood, it is in this verse and the two following it. As for messengership, it is taken from His saying: "What is that in your right hand, O Moses?" (20:17) and ends at His saying: "Go to Pharaoh, for he has rebelled" (20:24). Allah has explicitly stated that he was both a messenger and a prophet in His saying: **وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ مُوسى إِنَّهُ كانَ مُخْلَصاً وَكانَ رَسُولاً نَبِيًّا** — "And mention Moses in the Book. Indeed he was dedicated, and he was a messenger and a prophet" (19:51).

In the three verses encompassing prophethood, both pillars are mentioned: the pillar of belief (الاعتقاد) and the pillar of action (العمل). The foundations of belief are three: monotheism (التوحيد), prophethood (النبوة), and the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and the final judgment*. He mentioned monotheism and the Return and omitted prophethood because the speech was with the Prophet himself. As for the pillar of action, it was summarized—despite its details—in a single phrase: "So worship Me." Thus, the foundations of religion (أصول الدين) and its branches (فروعه) were completed in three verses.

His saying: "Indeed I am Allah—there is no god except Me" (إِنَّنِي أَنَا اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا) identified the One named by the Name through Himself, saying: "Indeed I am Allah," and He did not say: "Indeed Allah is I." This is because the requirement of Presence is that the description of a thing be identified by its essence, not its essence by its description. As the brothers of Joseph said when they recognized him: **إِنَّكَ لَأَنْتَ يُوسُفُ قالَ أَنَا يُوسُفُ وَهذا أَخِي** — "‘Are you indeed Joseph?’ He said, ‘I am Joseph, and this is my brother’" (12:90). Although the Name of Majesty (اسم الجلالة) is a proper noun for the Transcendent Essence, it conveys the meaning of the One named "Allah," for there is no path to the Sacred Essence itself. It is as if it were said: "I am the One who is called 'Allah'." The Speaker is present and witnessed, and the One named by the name "Allah" is as if it were ambiguous as to who He is; so it was said: "I am He." Furthermore, the Name of Majesty is a proper noun by predominance (علم بالغلبة) and is not devoid of a descriptive origin.

His saying: "there is no god except Me, so worship Me" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاعْبُدْنِي) — the word of monotheism is arranged upon His saying: "Indeed I am Allah" verbally, because it is arranged upon it in reality. For if He is the One from Whom everything begins, by Whom everything subsists, and to Whom everything returns, then it is not fitting that the submission of worship (العبادة) be shown to anyone but Him. He is the God worshipped in truth, there is no god besides Him. Therefore, He branched from that the command to worship Him, saying: "So worship Me."

His saying: "and maintain the prayer for My remembrance" (وَأَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِذِكْرِي) singled out prayer (الصلاة) for mention—which is an instance of mentioning the specific after the general to emphasize its importance—because prayer is the best action through which servile submission is represented, and through it, the remembrance (الذكر) of Allah, the Glorified, is realized just as the spirit is realized through its mold.

According to this meaning, His saying: "for My remembrance" (لِذِكْرِي) is the addition of the verbal noun to its object, and the *lam* is for causality (للتعليل), being connected to "maintain" (أقم). The sum of it is: "Realize your remembrance of Me through prayer," just as one says: "Eat to be full" or "Drink to be quenched." This is the meaning that first comes to mind from such a context.

There have been numerous opinions regarding His saying: "for My remembrance." It was said: it is connected to "maintain," as mentioned. It was said: to "prayer." It was said: to His saying: "So worship Me." Regarding the *lam*, it was said: it is for causality. It was said: for timing (للتوقيت), meaning: "Maintain the prayer at the time of My remembrance," or "at the time of remembering it if you forget it or it passes you by"; thus, it would be like the *lam* in His saying: **أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِدُلُوكِ الشَّمْسِ** — "Maintain the prayer from the sun's decline..." (17:78).

Regarding "remembrance" (الذكر), it was said: what is meant is the verbal remembrance contained within the prayer. It was said: the heart-centered remembrance that accompanies it and is realized through it, or which is arranged upon it and occurs through it as an effect from its cause, or the remembrance that precedes it. It was said: it refers to what is more general than both heart-centered and verbal remembrance.

Regarding the genitive construction (الإضافة), it was said: it is the addition of the verbal noun to its object. It was said: it is the addition of the verbal noun to its agent, meaning: "Pray so that I may remember you with praise and reward," or "Pray for My mention of it in the Heavenly Books and My command for it."

It was said: it implies the restriction (قصر) of the maintenance to the remembrance, meaning: "Maintain the prayer for the purpose of My remembrance, not for any other purpose such as a reward you hope for or a punishment you fear." It was said: there is no such restriction.

It was said: it implies the restriction of the added word to the word it is added to, meaning: "Maintain the prayer for My remembrance specifically, without showing off or mixing it with the remembrance of others." It was said: there is no indication of this from the perspective of the wording, even if it is true in itself.

It was said: what is meant by "remembrance" is the remembrance of the prayer itself, meaning: "Maintain the prayer upon remembering it or for the sake of remembering it." The speech would then be based on an omitted word, the original being: "for the remembrance of my prayer," or on the basis that the remembrance of prayer is a cause for the remembrance of Allah, so the effect was mentioned while the cause was intended—among other views. The resulting opinions range from the weak to the substantial. What is first understood is what we have presented.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ السَّاعَةَ آتِيَةٌ أَكادُ أُخْفِيها لِتُجْزى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما تَسْعى** — "Indeed the Hour is coming. I almost keep it hidden, so that every soul may be rewarded for what it strives" (20:15). This is a justification for His saying in the previous verse: "So worship Me." This does not contradict the fact that "So worship Me" was branched from the word of monotheism mentioned before it. For although the obligation of worshipping Him, the Exalted, is branched from His oneness in itself, it would have no effect were it not for the establishment of a Day in which man is rewarded for what he has done, and in which the doer of good is distinguished from the doer of evil, and the obedient from the disobedient. Otherwise, legislation would be futile and command and prohibition would be in vain, having no effect. Therefore, it was a decree of certain judgment, and the denial of doubt regarding it is repeated in His speech.

His saying: "I almost keep it hidden" (أَكادُ أُخْفِيها) — the apparent meaning of the absolute use of "hiding" is that it means: "I am close to hiding it and concealing it so that I do not inform about it at all, so that its occurrence may be more profound in its suddenness and more intense in its surprise." It shall not come except suddenly, as He, the Exalted, said: **لا تَأْتِيكُمْ إِلَّا بَغْتَةً** — "It will not come to you but suddenly" (7:187). Or: "I am close to not informing about it so that the sincere may be distinguished from others." For most people only worship Him, the Exalted, out of hope for His reward or fear of His punishment as a recompense for obedience and disobedience. The truest action is that which is for the sake of Allah, not out of greed for Paradise or fear of Hell. If the Day of Recompense were hidden and concealed, then those who perform the reality of worship would be distinguished from others.

It was said: the meaning of "I almost keep it hidden" is: "I am close to concealing it from Myself," which is an exaggeration in concealment. When someone wishes to exaggerate the concealment of something, he says: "I almost hid it from myself," meaning: "How then would I disclose it to others?" This has been attributed to a narration.

His saying: "so that every soul may be rewarded for what it strives" (لِتُجْزى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما تَسْعى) is connected to His saying: "is coming" (آتِيَةٌ), and the meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلا يَصُدَّنَّكَ عَنْها مَنْ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِها وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ فَتَرْدى** — "So do not let him who does not believe in it and follows his desires bar you from it, lest you should perish" (20:16). "Barring" (الصد) is turning away, and "perishing" (الردى) is destruction. The two pronouns in "from it" (عَنْها) and "in it" (بِها) refer to the Hour. The meaning of being barred from the Hour is being turned away from its remembrance in terms of its significance—which is that it is a day in which every soul is rewarded for what it strives. Likewise, the meaning of not believing in it is disbelieving in it in terms of its significance.

His saying: "and follows his desires" (وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ) is like an explanatory apposition (عطف تفسير) to His saying: "him who does not believe in it," meaning that the lack of belief in it is an instance of following desires. Since it is also suitable for justification, the speech implies the causality of desire for the lack of belief in it. From this, it is understood by implication that belief in the Hour is the Truth that opposes desire and saves one from destruction.

The sum of the verse's meaning is: Since the Hour is coming and the recompense is occurring, let not those who followed their desires—and thus came to disbelieve in it and turn away from the worship of their Lord—turn you away from belief in it and from its remembrance in terms of its significance. Let them not turn you away from it such that you turn away and thus perish.

Perhaps the use of the past tense in His saying: "and follows his desires" (وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ), while the word it is conjoined to is in the present tense, is to hint at the causality of following desire for the lack of belief.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما تِلْكَ بِيَمِينِكَ يا مُوسى** — "What is that in your right hand, O Moses?" (20:17). This is the beginning of the revelation of messengership (الرسالة), the revelation of prophethood (النبوة) having been completed in the three preceding verses. The interrogation is for affirmation (التقرير). He (peace be upon him) was asked about what was in his right hand—which was his staff—so that he might name it and mention its attributes. Thus, it would be clear that it was an inanimate object with no life, so that its transformation into a slithering snake would take its place in his soul (peace be upon him).

It appears that the object pointed to by "that" (تِلْكَ) is the stick or the wood. Were it not for that, the proper speech would have been: "What is that (ذلك)?" making the object pointed to "the thing" due to the feigned ignorance of it being a staff; otherwise, the interrogation would not be sound, as in His saying: **فَلَمَّا رَأَى الشَّمْسَ بازِغَةً قالَ هذا رَبِّي هذا أَكْبَرُ** — "When he saw the sun rising, he said, ‘This is my Lord! This is bigger!’" (6:78).

It is possible that the pointing with "that" (تِلْكَ) is to the staff, but not for the purpose of discovering its name and reality—which would make the interrogation futile—but for the purpose of having him mention its attributes and properties. This is supported by the prolixity (الإطناب) in Moses' speech (peace be upon him) in mentioning the qualities and properties of the staff. For when he heard the question about what was in his right hand—and it was a staff about which he had no doubt—he understood that what was required was the mention of its attributes. So he began by mentioning its name, then its attributes and properties. This is a method employed when one is asked about an obvious matter where ignorance is not expected. From this category is the explanation of His saying, the Exalted: **الْقارِعَةُ مَا الْقارِعَةُ وَما أَدْراكَ مَا الْقارِعَةُ يَوْمَ يَكُونُ النَّاسُ كَالْفَراشِ الْمَبْثُوثِ** — "The Catastrophe! What is the Catastrophe? What will show you what the Catastrophe is? [It is] the day when mankind will be like scattered moths" (101:1-4), and His saying: **الْحَاقَّةُ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ وَما أَدْراكَ مَا الْحَاقَّةُ** — "The Inevitable! What is the Inevitable? What will show you what the Inevitable is?" (69:1-3).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ هِيَ عَصايَ أَتَوَكَّؤُا عَلَيْها وَأَهُشُّ بِها عَلى غَنَمِي وَلِيَ فِيها مَآرِبُ أُخْرى** — "He said, ‘It is my staff. I lean upon it, and with it I beat down leaves for my sheep, and I have other uses for it’" (20:18). The "staff" (العصا) is well-known and is among the feminine nouns by usage. "Leaning" (التوكي والاتكاء) upon the staff is relying on it. "Beating down" (الهش) is striking the leaves of a tree with the staff so they fall for the sheep to eat. "Uses" (المآرب) is the plural of *ma'rabah*, meaning a need. The meaning of his having "uses" for it is the attachment of his needs to it insofar as it is a means of fulfilling them. The meaning of the verse is clear. Regarding his prolixity (peace be upon him) in lengthening the mention of the staff's attributes and properties, it was said: because the situation—being one of intimate discourse and private conversation with the Beloved—requires this, for speaking with the Beloved is delightful. Therefore, he first mentioned that it was his staff so that he could arrange its general benefits upon it; this is the subtle point in mentioning that it was his staff.

We have previously presented another reason for this interrogation and its answer, and the speech regarding it is not of the nature of prolixity, especially considering his grouping of all its other benefits in His saying: "and I have other uses for it."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ أَلْقِها يا مُوسى** — "He said, ‘Throw it down, O Moses!’" (20:19) until His saying, "**سِيرَتَهَا الْأُولى**" (its former state). "State" (السيرة) is the condition and way; it is originally a noun of kind for "walking" (*sayr*), like *jilsah* for a kind of sitting.

The Glorified commanded Moses to throw down his staff from his right hand, which is His saying: "He said, ‘Throw it down, O Moses!’" When he threw the staff, it became a snake moving with vigor and agility, which is something unexpected from an inanimate object with no life; this is His saying: **فَأَلْقاها فَإِذا هِيَ حَيَّةٌ تَسْعى** — "So he threw it down, and behold, it was a snake, slithering" (20:20). The Exalted described its slithering in another place in His speech by saying: **رَآها تَهْتَزُّ كَأَنَّها جَانٌّ** — "...he saw it quivering as if it were a snake..." (28:31). He also described the snake in another place by saying: **فَأَلْقى عَصاهُ فَإِذا هِيَ ثُعْبانٌ مُبِينٌ** — "So he threw down his staff, and behold, it was a manifest python" (7:107, 26:32). A "python" (الثعبان) is a large snake.

His saying: **قالَ خُذْها وَلا تَخَفْ سَنُعِيدُها سِيرَتَهَا الْأُولى** — "He said, ‘Take hold of it, and do not be afraid. We will restore it to its former state’" (20:21)—meaning its condition of being a staff—contains an indication of his fear (peace be upon him) of what he witnessed of the slithering snake. The Exalted narrated this in another place when He said: **فَلَمَّا رَأَى تَهْتَزُّ كَأَنَّها جَانٌّ وَلَّى مُدْبِراً وَلَمْ يُعَقِّبْ يا مُوسى أَقْبِلْ وَلا تَخَفْ** — "But when he saw it quivering as if it were a snake, he turned his back [to flee], and did not look back. ‘O Moses! Come forward and do not be afraid...’" (28:31). "Fear" (الخوف), which is taking the preliminaries of caution against evil, is different from "dread" (الخشية), which is the heart's being affected and agitated. Dread is a vice that contradicts the virtue of courage, unlike fear. It is permissible for prophets (peace be upon them) to experience fear, but not dread, as Allah the Exalted said: **الَّذِينَ يُبَلِّغُونَ رِسالاتِ اللهِ وَيَخْشَوْنَهُ وَلا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَداً إِلَّا اللهَ** — "Such as deliver the messages of Allah and fear Him, and fear no one except Allah" (33:39).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاضْمُمْ يَدَكَ إِلى جَناحِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضاءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوءٍ آيَةً أُخْرى** — "And draw your hand to your side: it will come out white, without any fault, as another sign" (20:22). "Drawing" (الضم) is gathering. "Side" (الجناح) refers to the wing of a bird, the hand, the upper arm, or the armpit. Perhaps the last meaning is intended, so that it resolves to His saying in another place: **أَدْخِلْ يَدَكَ فِي جَيْبِكَ** — "Insert your hand into your shirt-front..." (27:12). "Fault" (السوء) refers to any badness or ugliness; it was said that in the verse, it is a metaphor for leprosy. The meaning is: "Gather your hand under your armpit—meaning insert it into your shirt-front—it will come out white without leprosy or any other bad condition."

His saying: "as another sign" (آيَةً أُخْرى) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the pronoun in "it will come out." It contains an indication that the staff becoming a snake was the first sign, and the white hand is another sign. The Exalted said regarding this: **فَذانِكَ بُرْهانانِ مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِلى فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَائِهِ** — "These shall be two proofs from your Lord to Pharaoh and his elite" (28:32).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لِنُرِيَكَ مِنْ آياتِنَا الْكُبْرى** — "that We may show you some of Our greater signs" (20:23). The *lam* is for causality, and the sentence is connected to an omitted part, as if it were said: "We performed what We performed at your hand so that We might show you some of Our greater signs."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **اذْهَبْ إِلى فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ طَغى** — "Go to Pharaoh, for he has rebelled" (20:24). This is the command of messengership (الرسالة), and the preceding verses—"What is that in your right hand..." etc.—were a prelude to it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ رَبِّ اشْرَحْ لِي صَدْرِي** — "He said, ‘My Lord! Open my breast for me...’" (20:25) until His saying, "**إِنَّكَ كُنْتَ بِنا بَصِيراً**" — "...Indeed You have been watchful of us" (20:35). These verses—eleven in total—are the text of what Moses (peace be upon him) asked of his Lord when the decree of messengership was recorded for him. In their apparent meaning, they are linked to the matter of his messengership, for he was in greatest need of them for delivering the message to Pharaoh and his elite, for saving the Children of Israel, and for managing their affairs—not for the matter of prophethood.

This is supported by the fact that he did not ask for this after the completion of the matter of prophethood in the three preceding verses, but rather hastened to do so only after he was given the command: "Go to Pharaoh, for he has rebelled," which is the command of messengership.

Yes, the first four verses—"My Lord! Open my breast for me," etc.—are not devoid of some general connection to the matter of prophethood (النبوة), which is the reception of the beliefs of religion and its practical laws from the Divine Presence.

His saying: "My Lord! Open my breast for me" (رَبِّ اشْرَحْ لِي صَدْرِي). "Opening" (الشرح) is expansion. The sentence is an imaginary metaphor (استعارة تخييلية) and a metaphor by allusion (استعارة بالكناية). It is as if the human breast, in which the heart is sheltered, is a vessel that contains what comes to it by way of witnessing and perception, and then stores the secret within it. When a matter is great and difficult for a person, or beyond his capacity, the breast becomes narrow for it and cannot contain it, thus requiring expansion (انشراح) so that it may accommodate it.

Moses considered the matter of messengership that his Lord had decreed for him to be immense. He was aware of the power and might of the Coptic nation, at the head of which was the rebellious tyrant Pharaoh, who contested Allah's Lordship and proclaimed: "I am your most exalted lord." He also remembered the weakness and captivity of the Children of Israel among the people of Pharaoh, as well as their ignorance and intellectual decline. He seemed to see the hardships and calamities that this call would bring upon him, and witnessed the horrors and tragedies that would follow the delivery of this message. He was a man of little endurance, quick to be moved for the sake of Allah, rejecting injustice and refusing oppression, as evidenced by the story of his killing the Copt and his drawing water at the well of Midian. Furthermore, in his tongue—which is the only weapon for one who intends to call and deliver a message—there was a knot that might prevent him from clearly expressing what he wished to explain.

Therefore, he asked his Lord to solve these problems. He first asked Him to expand his breast for the burdens of messengership he was to bear and for the greatness and hardships he would face in his path of calling, saying: "My Lord! Open my breast for me."

Then he said: "and ease my task for me" (وَيَسِّرْ لِي أَمْرِي), referring to the task of messengership with which he was entrusted. He did not ask the Exalted to lighten his messengership or to descend somewhat from what He had first commanded him, so that he might be content with something less and the messengership would become easy in itself after having been grave. Rather, he asked that it be made easy for him, despite its inherent difficulty and danger. The proof for this is His saying: "for me" (لِي).

The point of proof is that His saying "for me," in this context, implies specificity (الاختصاص). It conveys the meaning of our saying: "And ease for me—I who have been placed in this position and entrusted with what I have been entrusted—my task which You have entrusted to me." It is well-known that the requirement of this request is the easing of the task relative to him, not its easing in itself. A similar point applies to His saying: "Open for me" (اشْرَحْ لِي); its meaning is: "Open for me—I who have been commanded with messengership, in the face of which are hardships and dislikes—my breast, so that it does not become narrow when they crowd upon me and overwhelm me." Had it been said: "My Lord, open the breast and ease the task," this subtle point would have been lost.

His saying: "and untie the knot from my tongue, that they may understand my speech" (وَاحْلُلْ عُقْدَةً مِنْ لِسانِي يَفْقَهُوا قَوْلِي) is another request of his, referring to a knot in his tongue. The indefiniteness in "a knot" (عُقْدَةً) is to indicate its type; it has an implied description hinted at by His saying: "that they may understand my speech," meaning: "a knot that prevents the understanding of my speech."

His saying: "and appoint for me a minister from my family, Aaron, my brother" (وَاجْعَلْ لِي وَزِيراً مِنْ أَهْلِي هارُونَ أَخِي) is another request of his, the fourth and last. "Minister" (الوزير) is of the form *fa'il* from *wizr* (with a *kasrah*), meaning a heavy burden; the minister is called a *wazir* because he bears the weight of the king's burden. It was also said: it is from *wazar* (with two *fathahs*), meaning a mountain to which one takes refuge; he is so named because the king takes refuge in his opinions and judgments.

In short, he asks his Lord to appoint for him a minister from his family and specifies that it be "Aaron, my brother." He asks for this because the matter has many sides and far-reaching aspects; Moses cannot undertake it alone. Rather, he needs a minister to share in it, who will undertake part of the task and thus lighten the burden for Moses in what this minister performs, and who will be a support for Moses in what Moses performs. This is the meaning of his saying—which is like an explanation for appointing him a minister—"strengthen my back by him, and make him associate in my task" (اشْدُدْ بِهِ أَزْرِي وَأَشْرِكْهُ فِي أَمْرِي).

The meaning of his saying: "and make him associate in my task" (وَأَشْرِكْهُ فِي أَمْرِي) is a request for association in a task that was specific to him, which is the delivery of what he received from his Lord at the very outset. This is what was specific to him, in which no one else shared, nor did he have the right to appoint a deputy for it. As for the delivery of the religion or any of its parts after it has been delivered through the Prophet, it is not something specific to the Prophet; rather, it is the duty of everyone who believes in him and knows something of the religion. It is for the scholar to deliver to the ignorant, and for the witness to deliver to the absent. There would be no meaning in asking for his brother's association with him in a matter that is not specific to him, but rather common to him, his brother, and everyone who believes in him—such as guidance, teaching, explanation, and delivery. Thus, it becomes clear that the meaning of associating him in his task is that he should undertake the delivery of some of what is revealed to him from his Lord on his behalf, and other matters specific to him from Allah, such as the obligation of obedience and the authority of his word.

As for association in prophethood (النبوة) specifically, in the sense of receiving revelation from Allah, the Glorified, Moses did not fear being alone in that such that he would ask for a partner. He only feared being alone in the delivery and the management of affairs in saving the Children of Israel and what pertains to that. This has been narrated from Moses himself in His saying: **وَأَخِي هارُونُ هُوَ أَفْصَحُ مِنِّي لِساناً فَأَرْسِلْهُ مَعِي رِدْءاً يُصَدِّقُنِي** — "My brother Aaron is more eloquent than me in speech. So send him with me as a helper to confirm me" (28:34).

Furthermore, it has been established through the paths of both groups [Sunni and Shi'a] that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) prayed with this very prayer, using its words, on behalf of Ali (peace be upon him), and he was not a prophet.

His saying: "so that we may glorify You much, and remember You much" (كَيْ نُسَبِّحَكَ كَثِيراً وَنَذْكُرَكَ كَثِيراً). The context—having mentioned in the end the glorification of both of them together and the remembrance of both of them together—indicates that the sentence is the end (غاية) for appointing Aaron as his minister. For the glorification of both of them together and their remembrance together has no connection to the contents of the previous prayers, which were the expansion of his breast, the easing of his task, and the untying of the knot from his tongue. It follows that what is meant by glorification (التسبيح) and remembrance (الذكر) is their joint sanctification of Allah, the Glorified, and their joint remembrance of Him among the people publicly, not in their private states or secretly in their hearts; for that also has no connection to appointing him a minister. Rather, the meaning is that they should glorify Him and remember Him together among the people in their gatherings, assemblies, and any council of theirs in which they reside or are present. Thus, the call to belief in Allah and the rejection of partners would increase.

By this, the end of the context returns to its beginning, as if he were saying: "The matter is grave. This tyrant, his elite, and his nation have been deceived by their might and authority. Polytheism and idolatry have taken deep root in their hearts and have made them forget the remembrance of Allah entirely. The eyes of the Children of Israel have been filled with what they witness of Pharaoh's might and the power of his elite; their hearts are bewildered by the onslaught of the people of Pharaoh, and their souls are terrified by their authority, so they have forgotten Allah and remember none but the tyrant. Therefore, this matter—the matter of messengership and the call—in its success and progress, is in dire need of Your sanctification by the frequent denial of partners, and Your remembrance as Lord and God among them frequently, so that they may gain insight and believe. This is a matter I cannot handle alone, so appoint Aaron as a minister for me, support me by him, and associate him in my task, so that we may glorify You much and remember You much, that perhaps the effort may succeed and the call may be of benefit."

Through this explanation, the reason for the connection of this end—meaning His saying: "so that we may glorify You..." etc.—to what preceded it becomes clear.

Second: The reason for the occurrence of His saying: "much" (كَثِيراً) twice, and that it is not a repetition at all, since each of glorification and remembrance must be "much" in itself. Had it been said: "so that we may glorify You and remember You much," it would have implied the muchness of both combined, which is not what is intended.

Third: The reason for placing glorification before remembrance. For what is meant by glorification is sanctifying Him, the Exalted, from partners by refuting the divinity of gods besides Allah and nullifying their lordship, so that the call to belief in Allah alone—which is what is meant by remembrance—may take its proper place. Thus, glorification is of the nature of removing the obstacle, which precedes the effect of the cause. Other reasons for these particularities have been mentioned in longer works, but there is no benefit in them or in citing them.

His saying: "Indeed You have been watchful of us" (إِنَّكَ كُنْتَ بِنا بَصِيراً) is, in its apparent meaning, a justification like a proof for His saying: "so that we may glorify You much..." etc. That is: "You have been watchful of me and my brother since You created us and made Yourself known to us, and You know that we have never ceased worshipping You with glorification and remembrance, striving and exerting ourselves in that. If You appoint him as a minister for me, support me by him, and associate him in my task, the matter of the call will be completed, and we will glorify You much and remember You much." What is meant by "us" (بِنا) according to this is he and his brother. It is also possible that the pronoun in "us" refers to his family, the meaning being: "You have been watchful of us, the people of the house, that we are people of glorification and remembrance. If You appoint Aaron my brother—who is of my family—as a minister for me, we will glorify You much and remember You much." This view is better than the previous one because it also accounts for the subtle point in mentioning "family" in his previous saying: "and appoint for me a minister from my family, Aaron, my brother." Understand this well.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ قَدْ أُوتِيتَ سُؤْلَكَ يا مُوسى** — "He said, ‘O Moses, your request has been granted’" (20:36). This is an answer to all his prayers, and it is a performative (إنشاء) similar to what passed in His saying: "I have chosen you, so listen to what is revealed."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلَيْكَ مَرَّةً أُخْرى** — "Certainly We have favored you another time" (20:37) until His saying, "**كَيْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُها وَلا تَحْزَنَ**" — "...so that her eyes might be consoled and she should not grieve" (20:40). He, the Exalted, reminds him of another favor He bestowed upon him before choosing him for prophethood and messengership and granting his request. This was His favor upon him when he was born. Some soothsayers had informed Pharaoh that a child would be born among the Children of Israel at whose hands the end of his kingdom would occur. So Pharaoh ordered the killing of every child born among them; they would kill the male infants until, when Moses was born, Allah revealed to his mother that she should not fear and should suckle him. If she feared for him from Pharaoh's workers and henchmen, she should cast him into a chest and cast it into the Nile; the river would cast it to the shore near Pharaoh's palace, and he would take him and adopt him as a son—for he had no offspring—and would not kill him. Then Allah would return him to her.

She did as was revealed to her. When the chest flowed with the current of the Nile, she sent a daughter of hers—Moses' sister—to track his news. She went around Pharaoh's palace until she found a group seeking, by Pharaoh's command, a wet-nurse to suckle Moses. Moses' sister directed them to her mother, so they sought her to suckle him. Thus, she took her child, her eyes were consoled, and Allah's promise was proven true. This was a great favor from Him upon Moses.

His saying: "Certainly We have favored you another time" (وَلَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلَيْكَ مَرَّةً أُخْرى) is an expression of favor for what He did for him at the beginning of his life. The context has changed from the first-person singular to the first-person plural because the situation is one of manifesting greatness. it informs of the manifestation of His perfect power in frustrating the efforts of the tyrant Pharaoh, nullifying his plot to extinguish Allah's light, turning his scheme back upon him, and raising His enemy in his own lap. As for the station of calling Moses and speaking to him, where He said: "O Moses! Indeed I am your Lord," etc., the first-person singular context is more appropriate for it.

His saying: "when We revealed to your mother what was to be revealed" (إِذْ أَوْحَيْنا إِلى أُمِّكَ ما يُوحى). What is meant by it is inspiration (الإلهام), which is a kind of casting into the heart while awake or asleep. Revelation (الوحي) in His speech, the Exalted, is not restricted to the revelation of prophethood, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَأَوْحى رَبُّكَ إِلَى النَّحْلِ** — "And your Lord revealed to the bee..." (16:68). As for the revelation of prophethood, women do not receive prophethood, nor is it revealed to them in that sense. He, the Exalted, said: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ إِلَّا رِجالاً نُوحِي إِلَيْهِمْ مِنْ أَهْلِ الْقُرى** — "We did not send [any apostles] before you except men from the people of the towns, to whom We revealed" (12:109). His saying: "‘Cast him into the chest...’" until the end of the verse is the content of what was revealed to Moses' mother. The word "that" (*an*) is for explanation (التفسير). It was said: it is a particle of infinitive (مصدرية) connected to "revealed," the implied meaning being: "We revealed that you should cast him." It was said: it is a particle of infinitive and the sentence is a substitute (بدل) for "what was to be revealed."

The "chest" (التابوت) is a box or something similar. "Casting" (القذف) is placing and throwing. It is as if the first "casting" in the verse is in the first sense [placing], and the second "casting" is in the second sense [throwing]. It is possible that both are in the second sense, with the consideration that placing the child in the chest and throwing it into the river is a "casting" and a "throwing" of him without regard for his state. The "river" (اليم) is the sea; it was said: the sweet sea [large river]. The "shore" (الساحل) is the bank of the sea and its side on the land. "Favor" (الصنع والصنيعة) is an act of kindness.

His saying: "let the river cast him" (فَلْيُلْقِهِ الْيَمُّ) is a command used to indicate the certainty of its occurrence. Its meaning is: "We commanded the river with that by a creative command (أمراً تكوينياً), so it is an inevitable, decreed occurrence." Likewise, His saying: "he will be picked up by an enemy of Mine..." etc., is a recompense arranged upon this command.

The meaning of the two verses is: "When We revealed and inspired your mother with what is revealed and inspired, which is: place him—or cast him—into the chest, which is the box, then cast it into the river and the sea, which is the Nile. It is decreed from Us that the sea shall cast him to the shore and the bank, and an enemy of Mine and an enemy of his shall take him—namely Pharaoh, because he was an enemy of Allah by claiming divinity and an enemy of Moses by killing children, and he was a child. This is what We revealed to your mother."

His saying: "And I draped you with a love from Me, and that you might be reared under My eye" (وَأَلْقَيْتُ عَلَيْكَ مَحَبَّةً مِنِّي وَلِتُصْنَعَ عَلى عَيْنِي). The apparent context is that this section, until His saying: "and she should not grieve," is a second section following the previous one and completing it. The whole is an explanation of the favor referred to by His saying: "Certainly We have favored you another time."

The first section narrates the revelation to his mother to cast him into the chest and then into the river so that he would end up with Pharaoh and be taken by the enemy of Allah and his enemy. The second section narrates the draping of love upon him so that Pharaoh would turn away from killing him and treat him with kindness, until the matter ended with his return to his mother and his settling in her lap, so that her eyes might be consoled and she should not grieve. Allah had promised her that, as He said in Surah al-Qasas: **فَرَدَدْناهُ إِلى أُمِّهِ كَيْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُها وَلا تَحْزَنَ وَلِتَعْلَمَ أَنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌّ** — "So We restored him to his mother, that her eye might be consoled and she should not grieve, and that she might know that Allah’s promise is true" (28:13). The necessary implication of this meaning is that the sentence—meaning His saying: "And I draped you..." etc.—is conjoined to His saying: "when We revealed to your mother."

The meaning of draping a love from Him upon him is that he became such that everyone who saw him loved him, as if the divine love had settled upon him; no look of an observer fell upon him but that love attached to his heart and drew him toward Moses. In the speech, there is an imaginary metaphor (استعارة تخييلية), and in the indefiniteness of "love" (محبة), there is an indication of its magnificence and the strangeness of its nature.

The *lam* in His saying: "and that you might be reared under My eye" (وَلِتُصْنَعَ عَلى عَيْنِي) is for purpose (للغرض). The sentence is conjoined to an omitted part, the implied meaning being: "I draped you with a love from Me for such and such reasons, and so that you might be treated with kindness under My eye—meaning in My sight—for I am with you, watching your state and never heedless of you, due to My increased care for you and My compassion upon you." It was also said: what is meant by His saying: "and that you might be reared under My eye" is the kindness shown to him by returning him to his mother and placing his upbringing in her lap.

In any case, this language—which is the language of perfect care and compassion—suits the first-person singular context; therefore, it shifted to it from the first-person plural.

His saying: "When your sister walked up and said, ‘Shall I guide you to someone who will take care of him?’ So We restored you to your mother, that her eyes might be consoled and she should not grieve" (إِذْ تَمْشِي أُخْتُكَ فَتَقُولُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلى مَنْ يَكْفُلُهُ فَرَجَعْناكَ إِلى أُمِّكَ كَيْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُها وَلا تَحْزَنَ). The adverbial phrase (الظرف)—according to what the context provides—is connected to His saying: "and that you might be reared." The meaning is: "And I draped you with a love from Me such that everyone who saw you loved you for such and such reasons, and so that you might be treated with kindness in My sight and under My watch at the time when your sister was walking to track your news and see what would be done with you. She found Pharaoh's workers seeking a wet-nurse to suckle you, so she said to them—the present tense in the verb is to narrate a past state—offering to them: ‘Shall I guide you to someone who will take care of him through nursing and suckling?’ So We returned you to your mother so that she might be delighted and not grieve."

His saying: "So We restored you" (فَرَجَعْناكَ) in the first-person plural is a return to the previous context, which is the first-person plural, and it is not a shift (التفات).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقَتَلْتَ نَفْساً فَنَجَّيْناكَ مِنَ الْغَمِّ** — "And you killed a soul, and We delivered you from anguish..." (20:40) until the end of the verse. This is an indication of another favor or favors attached to the two previous favors. It refers to the story of his killing the Copt (peace be upon him) in Egypt, the conspiracy of the elite to kill him, his flight from Egypt, his marriage there to the daughter of the Prophet Shu'ayb, and his remaining with him among the people of Midian for ten years as a hired hand tending Shu'ayb's sheep. The story is mentioned in detail in Surah al-Qasas.

His saying: "And you killed a soul" refers to his killing the Copt in Egypt. His saying: "and We delivered you from anguish" refers to what he feared of the elite of Pharaoh's people killing him; so Allah brought him out to the land of Midian. When Shu'ayb brought him in, and he came to him and narrated the stories to him, he said: "Do not be afraid. You have been delivered from the wrongdoing people."

His saying: "and We tried you with [various] trials" (وَفتَنَّاكَ فُتُوناً)—meaning We afflicted you and tested you with affliction and testing. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The origin of *fatn* (الفتن) is placing gold in the fire to manifest its quality from its dross. It was used for placing a human in the fire, as He said: **يَوْمَ هُمْ عَلَى النَّارِ يُفْتَنُونَ** — 'The day they are tried at the Fire' (51:13), and **ذُوقُوا فِتْنَتَكُمْ** — 'Taste your trial!' (51:14), meaning your punishment." He said: "Sometimes they call that which results in punishment a 'trial' (فتنة), so it is used in such as His saying: **أَلا فِي الْفِتْنَةِ سَقَطُوا** — 'Look! They have [already] fallen into temptation' (9:49). And sometimes it is used for testing, such as: 'and We tried you with [various] trials.' Trial (الفتنة) was made like affliction (البلاء) in that both are used for what a human is pushed into of hardship and ease, though they are more apparent in meaning and more frequent in usage regarding hardship. He said regarding both: **وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً** — 'We will test you with good and ill by way of trial' (21:35)." This concludes the relevant part of his speech.

His saying: "Then you stayed for several years among the people of Midian" (فَلَبِثْتَ سِنِينَ فِي أَهْلِ مَدْيَنَ) is branched from the trial. His saying: "Then you came [here] as ordained, O Moses" (ثُمَّ جِئْتَ عَلى قَدَرٍ يا مُوسى). It is not unlikely to understand from the context that what is meant by "ordained" (القدر) is the "attained measure" (المقدر), which is what he attained of knowledge and action from the trials that came upon him in his deliverance from anguish by leaving Egypt and his stay among the people of Midian.

According to this, the whole of His saying: "And you killed a soul, and We delivered you... until... O Moses" is of a single piece, which is that he was tried with trial after trial until he came according to a measure (قدر), which is what he acquired of the actuality of perfection.

Perhaps the objection to counting trials (الفتن) as a favor is answered by saying that "trials" here means "purification" (التخليص), like the purification of gold by fire. Or it was answered that its being a favor is in consideration of the reward arranged upon it. Both views are based on separating His saying: "Then you stayed..." until the end of the verse from what preceded it. Therefore, some said: what is meant by the "trial" is the hardship Moses endured after leaving Egypt until he settled in Midian, due to the *fa* of branching (فاء التفريع) in His saying: "Then you stayed for several years among the people of Midian," which indicates the temporal following of the stay after the trial. However, the *fa* only indicates branching, and it is not necessary that it always be a temporal branching.

Some said: "ordained" (القدر) means "predestination" (التقدير), and the meaning is: "Then you came to the land of Egypt according to what We predestined." Then an objection was raised against taking "ordained" (القدر) in the sense of "measure" (المقدار), stating that the well-known form of *qadar* in this sense is with a *sukun* on the *dal* [i.e., *qadr*], not with a *fathah*. However, *qadr* and *qadar*—with *sukun* and *fathah* on the *dal*—are one and the same, as they have explicitly stated, like *na'l* and *na'al*. Furthermore, *qadar* in the sense of "measure" (المقدار)—as we have presented—is more suited to the context, or even determined. Other meanings were mentioned for his coming "according to a measure," but they are trivial and of no benefit.

The mention of the favors was concluded with the calling of Moses (peace be upon him) to increase his honor.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاصْطَنَعْتُكَ لِنَفْسِي** — "And I have chosen you for Myself" (20:41). "Choosing" (الاصطناع) is of the form *ifta'al* from *sun'* (صنع), meaning kindness—according to what they mentioned. It is said: "he made him" (*sana'ahu*), meaning he showed him kindness, and "he chose him" (*istana'ahu*), meaning he realized his kindness toward him and established it within him. It was narrated from al-Qaffal that the meaning of *istina'* is that it is said: "So-and-so chose so-and-so" when he shows him kindness until he is attributed to him, and it is said: "This is the protégé (صنيع) and trainee of so-and-so." End quote.

According to this, the meaning of His "choosing" him resolves to His dedicating him (إخلاصه) to Himself. The position of His saying: "for Myself" (لِنَفْسِي) appears most perfectly. As for the first meaning, it is more appropriate, considering the context, that "choosing" (الاصطناع) should imply the meaning of "dedication" (الإخلاص). The meaning in any case is: "I have made you pure for Myself in the blessings you possess; all of them are from Me and My kindness, and no one else shares in you with Me. Thus, you are for Me, dedicated." This corresponds to His saying: **وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ مُوسى إِنَّهُ كانَ مُخْلَصاً** — "And mention Moses in the Book. Indeed he was dedicated" (19:51).

From here, it becomes clear that the saying of some: "What is meant by choosing is selection (الاختيار), and the meaning of selecting him for Himself is making him a proof between Him and His creation—his speech being His speech and his call being His call," as well as the saying of others: "What is meant by 'for Myself' is for My revelation and My messengership," and the saying of yet others: "for My love"—all of these are instances of restriction without a restrictor.

It also appears that His choosing him for Himself is woven into the thread of the mentioned favors; rather, it is the greatest of blessings. It is possible that it is conjoined to His saying: "you came as ordained" as an explanatory apposition.

The objection to this meaning—that the interposition of the call between it and the mentioned favors does not suit its being woven into their thread, as Fakhr al-Din al-Razi mentioned in his *Tafsir*—and that it is better to make it a preparation for sending him to Pharaoh with the partnership of his brother in his task, is refuted.

For the interposition of the call is not restricted in its reason to what was mentioned. Perhaps the reason for it is to honor him with increased subtlety and to draw him near to the station of intimacy, so that it may be a preparation for the shift back from the first-person plural to the first-person singular in His saying: "And I have chosen you for Myself."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **اذْهَبْ أَنْتَ وَأَخُوكَ بِآياتِي وَلا تَنِيا فِي ذِكْرِي** — "Go, you and your brother, with My signs, and do not flag in My remembrance" (20:42). This is a renewal of the previous command addressed to Moses alone in His saying: "Go to Pharaoh, for he has rebelled," with the change of what is within it by joining Moses' brother to him. This was due to the change in the situation by the granting of Moses' request to associate Aaron in his task; thus, the address was directed a second time to both of them together.

He commanded them to go with His "signs" (آياتِي), though at that time only two signs had been given. This is a beautiful promise that he would be supported by others, and they would be given when necessary. As for the saying that what is meant is the two signs and that the plural is sometimes applied to two, or that each of the two signs resolves into many signs—these are views that should not be relied upon.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تَنِيا فِي ذِكْرِي** (and do not flag in My remembrance) is a prohibition against flagging (الوني) — *which is to become languid or faint*. It is more appropriate to the preceding context that "remembrance" (الذكر) here refers to the call to faith in Him, the Exalted, alone, rather than remembrance in the sense of turning toward Him with the heart or tongue, as has been suggested.

His saying, the Exalted: **اذْهَبا إِلى فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ طَغى فَقُولا لَهُ قَوْلاً لَيِّناً لَعَلَّهُ يَتَذَكَّرُ أَوْ يَخْشى** (Go, both of you, to Pharaoh, for he has indeed rebelled. Speak to him in a soft manner; maybe he will take admonition or fear [Allah].) He joined them both in the command a second time, addressing Musa and Harun together, just as He did in the preceding prohibition in His saying: **وَلا تَنِيا** (and do not flag). He paved the way for this by joining Harun to Musa in His saying: **اذْهَبْ أَنْتَ وَأَخُوكَ** (Go, you and your brother). It is not unlikely that this is a narration of another direct address and a dialogue that took place between Him, the Exalted, and His two messengers—together or separately—after that initial encounter. This is supported by the context of His subsequent saying: **قالا رَبَّنا إِنَّنا نَخافُ أَنْ يَفْرُطَ عَلَيْنا** (They said, ‘Our Lord! We fear lest he should overstep the bounds against us...’) etc.

The meaning of His saying: **فَقُولا لَهُ قَوْلاً لَيِّناً** (Speak to him in a soft manner) is the prohibition against speaking to him with harshness or violence, and this is among the most obligatory etiquettes of the call (الدعوة).

His saying: **لَعَلَّهُ يَتَذَكَّرُ أَوْ يَخْشى** (maybe he will take admonition or fear [Allah]) expresses the hope for his admonition or fear. This hope is established by the context of the dialogue, not by Him, the Exalted, who is the Knower of all that shall be. Admonition (التذكر) is the reflexive effect of reminding (التذكير); thus, it means acceptance and commitment to what the proof of the reminder necessitates and faith in it. Fear (الخشية) is one of the precursors to acceptance and faith. Thus, the ultimate meaning is: "Perhaps he will believe, or come close to it, and thereby grant some of what you ask of him."

Some who hold the view that Pharaoh's faith was accepted at the time of drowning have used this verse as evidence for his faith, based on the premise that "maybe" (لعل) when issued from Allah is "obligatory of occurrence" (واجب الوقوع), as has been attributed to Ibn Abbas and the early exegetes. Thus, the verse would indicate the certain occurrence of one of the two matters: admonition or fear, which is the pivot of salvation.

However, this is rejected. The terms "perhaps" (عسى) and "maybe" (لعل) in the speech of the Exalted only indicate what they indicate in the speech of others, which is hope (الترجي). The only difference is that the state of hope does not reside in Him—exalted is He above ignorance and sanctified—but rather resides in the context. This means that anyone who stands in this position and observes the various aspects of the situation would understand that it is hoped that such-and-such will occur. In the speech of others, hope may reside within the speaker himself, or it may reside in the context of the address.

Imam al-Razi stated in his *Tafsir* that no one knows the secret of His sending [messengers] to Pharaoh, despite His knowledge that he would not believe, except Allah; and there is no way in this and similar matters except submission and the cessation of objection.

This is quite strange. If the "secret of sending" refers to the validity of commanding something while knowing the impossibility of its external occurrence, then the impossibility or necessity of a thing's occurrence is only the state of the act in relation to its complete cause (العلة التامة) — *which consists of the agent and all other external factors involved in its existence*. A command (الأمر), however, does not relate to the act in terms of its state relative to all parts of its complete cause; rather, it relates to it in terms of its state relative to the agent (الفاعل), who is but one part of its complete cause. The ratio of the act or its absence to the agent is always one of possibility (الإمكان), because the agent is an incomplete cause (علة ناقصة) that does not necessitate the existence or non-existence of the act. Thus, the sending and the call, as well as the command, are valid in relation to Pharaoh because responding and obeying were, in relation to his own self, volitional and possible, even if they were impossible and precluded in relation to him when joined with all other hindering factors. This is the response of those who uphold free will (الاختيار). As for the determinists (المجبرة)—and he [al-Razi] is among them—the doubt spreads for them to all instances of religious obligations due to the universality of determinism (الجبر). They have answered this, according to their claim, by saying that the obligation is formal (صوري), intended to establish the proof (تمام الحجة) and sever any excuse.

If the "secret of sending" despite the knowledge that he will not believe refers to the benefit resulting from it so that it is removed from the realm of futility, then the true call (الدعوة الحقة), just as it produces its effect in one group by perfecting them in the direction of felicity (السعادة), likewise produces its effect in others by perfecting their wretchedness (شقائهم). The Exalted said: **وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ ما هُوَ شِفاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلا يَزِيدُ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا خَساراً** — **We send down in the Quran that which is a cure and mercy for the believers, and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss** (Al-Isra: 82). If the perfection in the direction of wretchedness were cancelled, the trial (الامتحان) therein would be futile; thus, the proof would not be established, nor would the excuse be severed. If the proof were not established in one direction and was invalidated, it would not be effective in the other direction, and this is self-evident.

His saying, the Exalted: **قالا رَبَّنا إِنَّنا نَخافُ أَنْ يَفْرُطَ عَلَيْنا أَوْ أَنْ يَطْغى** (They said, ‘Our Lord! We fear lest he should overstep the bounds against us or act with insolence.’) Overstepping (الفرط) means to precede or hasten. What is meant by it here, by the context of its contrast with insolence (الطغيان), is that he might hasten the punishment and not be patient until the completion of the call and the manifestation of the miraculous sign (الآية المعجزة). The meaning of "acting with insolence" (أن يطغى) is that he might exceed his limit in oppression, meeting the call by intensifying the torment of the Children of Israel and daring to violate the sanctity of the Divine Presence in a way he did not dare before the call. Attributing fear to the two of them is acceptable, as previously discussed in the commentary on His saying: **قالَ خُذْها وَلا تَخَفْ** (He said, ‘Take it and do not fear’).

An objection was raised against the verse: that the saying of the Exalted in another place to Musa, in response to his request to associate his brother in his affair: **سَنَشُدُّ عَضُدَكَ بِأَخِيكَ وَنجْعَلُ لَكُما سُلْطاناً فَلا يَصِلُونَ إِلَيْكُما** — **He said, ‘We will strengthen your arm by means of your brother, and invest both of you with such authority that they will not be able to touch you’** (Al-Qasas: 35), indicates that security was granted to them in a position prior to this one, due to His saying: "We will strengthen your arm by means of your brother." Therefore, there would be no meaning in them expressing fear after that.

The response is that their fear previously was for their own lives, as evidenced by Musa's saying there: **وَلَهُمْ عَلَيَّ ذَنْبٌ فَأَخافُ أَنْ يَقْتُلُونِ** — **and they have a charge against me, so I fear they will kill me** (Ash-Shu'ara: 14). Whereas what is in this verse is fear from them for the sake of the call, as previously mentioned.

Furthermore, it is possible that this fear recounted in the verse is the fear Musa felt previously during the intimate discourse (المناجاة), and the fear Harun felt after the command reached him; thus, they were gathered and joined together in this instance. We have already mentioned the possibility that His saying: **اذْهَبا إِلى فِرْعَوْنَ** (Go, both of you, to Pharaoh) until the end of the verses, is a narration of their speech in more than one instance.

His saying, the Exalted: **قالَ لا تَخافا إِنَّنِي مَعَكُما أَسْمَعُ وَأَرى** (He said, ‘Do not be afraid, for I am indeed with you, and I hear and see.’) That is: do not fear his overstepping or insolence; I am present with you, hearing what is said and seeing what is done, so I shall help you and not forsake you. It is an assurance through the promise of victory. Thus, His saying: **لا تَخافا** (Do not be afraid) is the assurance, and His saying: **إِنَّنِي مَعَكُما أَسْمَعُ وَأَرى** (for I am indeed with you, and I hear and see) is the justification for the assurance by way of presence, hearing, and seeing. This is the evidence that the sentence is an allusion (كناية) to oversight (المراقبة) and victory (النصرة); otherwise, presence and knowledge themselves encompass all things and states.

Some have used this verse as evidence that hearing (السمع) and sight (البصر) are two attributes additional to knowledge (العلم), based on the premise that His saying: **إِنَّنِي مَعَكُما** (I am indeed with you) indicates knowledge, and if **أَسْمَعُ وَأَرى** (I hear and see) also indicated it, it would necessitate repetition (التكرار), which is contrary to the original principle.

This is among the weakest of arguments. Firstly: because you have learned that the meaning of **إِنَّنِي مَعَكُما** is presence and witnessing (الشهادة), which is other than knowledge.

Secondly: because certain proofs (البراهين اليقينية) have been established for the identity (العينية) — *the essential oneness* — of the essential attributes (الصفات الذاتية), which are Life, Power, Knowledge, Hearing, and Sight, with one another, and the totality of them with the Essence (الذات). No probabilistic linguistic appearance (ظهور لفظي ظني) that contradicts certainty can ever be established.

Thirdly: because the matter is among the fundamental principles of gnosis (أصول المعارف), in which nothing but certain knowledge is relied upon. Thus, completing the proof with something like the "original principle of non-repetition" is as you see [flawed].

His saying, the Exalted: **فَأْتِياهُ فَقُولا إِنَّا رَسُولا رَبِّكَ** (So go to him and say, ‘We are the messengers of your Lord...’) until the end of the verse. He renewed the command for them to go to Pharaoh after assuring them and promising them protection and victory, and He clarified the entirety of the message they were charged with. This was to call Pharaoh to faith and to cease the torment of the Children of Israel and send them with them. Every time a state shifted in the dialogue, the command was renewed according to what was appropriate. This is seen in His first saying to Musa: **اذْهَبْ إِلى فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ طَغى** (Go to Pharaoh, for he has indeed rebelled); then His second saying when Musa mentioned his requests and they were granted: **اذْهَبْ أَنْتَ وَأَخُوكَ** (Go, you and your brother) and **اذْهَبا إِلى فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ طَغى** (Go, both of you, to Pharaoh, for he has indeed rebelled); then His saying when they mentioned their fear and were answered with security: **فَأْتِياهُ فَقُولا** (So go to him and say...) etc. In this is the detail of what they were to say to him.

His saying: **فَأْتِياهُ فَقُولا إِنَّا رَسُولا رَبِّكَ** (So go to him and say, ‘We are the messengers of your Lord’) is the conveyance that they are the messengers of Allah. In His subsequent saying: **وَالسَّلامُ عَلى مَنِ اتَّبَعَ الْهُدى** (and peace be upon him who follows the guidance) etc., is the call to the remaining parts of faith.

His saying: **فَأَرْسِلْ مَعَنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ وَلا تُعَذِّبْهُمْ** (so let the Children of Israel go with us, and do not torture them) is a secondary obligation (تكليف فرعي) directed at Pharaoh.
