His saying: **قَدْ جِئْناكَ بِآيَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكَ** (We have certainly brought you a sign from your Lord) is a reliance upon a proof that establishes their messengership. In the indefiniteness (تنكير) of "a sign" (آية) there is a silence regarding the number and an indication of the magnificence of its matter, the greatness of its status, and the clarity of its indication.

His saying: **وَالسَّلامُ عَلى مَنِ اتَّبَعَ الْهُدى** (and peace be upon him who follows the guidance) is like a parting salutation, indicating the completion of the message and clarifying the summary of what the religious call contains: that safety (السلامة) is spread over him who follows the guidance, and felicity is for him who is guided; thus, he will not encounter in the course of his life any disliked thing he abhors, neither in this world nor in the Hereafter.

His saying: **إِنَّا قَدْ أُوحِيَ إِلَيْنا أَنَّ الْعَذابَ عَلى مَنْ كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّى** (Indeed it has been revealed to us that the punishment shall be for him who denies and turns away) is in the position of justifying the preceding. That is: we only offer peace to the guided because Allah, the Glorified, has revealed to us that the punishment—which is the opposite of peace—is upon him who denies the signs of Allah—or the true call which is the guidance—and turns away and disregards it.

In the context of these two verses, there is a manifest disdain for Pharaoh's affair, for the worldly ornaments he adorned himself with, and for the pride and arrogance he displayed. It was said: **فَأْتِياهُ** (come to him) and not "go to him," for "coming" to a thing implies a closer contact than "going" to it, and coming to Pharaoh—who was the King of Egypt and the god of the Copts—was not that easy or simple. It was said: **فَقُولا** (and say) and not "say to him," as if he is not given importance. It was said: **إِنَّا رَسُولا رَبِّكَ** (We are the messengers of your Lord) and **بِآيَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكَ** (with a sign from your Lord), thus striking his ears twice with the fact that he has a Lord, he who used to cry out: **أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلى** (I am your exalted lord). It was said: **وَالسَّلامُ عَلى مَنِ اتَّبَعَ الْهُدى** (and peace be upon him who follows the guidance) without addressing him directly; similar to this is His saying: **أَنَّ الْعَذابَ عَلى مَنْ كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّى** (that the punishment shall be for him who denies and turns away) without direct address.

All of this is most appropriate in light of what appears from the tone of His saying, the Exalted: **لا تَخافا إِنَّنِي مَعَكُما أَسْمَعُ وَأَرى** (Do not be afraid, for I am indeed with you, and I hear and see) of perfect encompassment, might, and power that nothing can withstand.

Despite this, there is nothing in what they were commanded to address him with—from their saying: "We are the messengers of your Lord" until the end of the two verses—that constitutes harshness in speech or a departure from the "soft manner" (لين القول) they were first commanded with. For that is the truth of the word which must strike Pharaoh's ears without flattery, without awe, and without being affected by the appearance of his false authority and deceptive might.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): regarding His saying: **آتِيكُمْ مِنْها بِقَبَسٍ** (I may bring you a brand from it) — he said: "I may bring you a brand of fire to warm yourselves from the cold." **أَوْ أَجِدُ عَلَى النَّارِ هُدىً** (or find some guidance at the fire) — he had lost the way, so he said: "Or I may find a path at the fire."

In *al-Faqih*: Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) was asked about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **فَاخْلَعْ نَعْلَيْكَ إِنَّكَ بِالْوادِ الْمُقَدَّسِ طُوىً** (so take off your sandals; you are indeed in the sacred valley of Tuwa). He said: "They were made from the skin of a dead donkey."

**I say:** It was also narrated in the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, both as a disconnected (مرسلاً) and an unattributed (مضمراً) report. This meaning was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Abd al-Razzaq, al-Faryabi, Abd ibn Humayd, and Ibn Abi Hatim from Ali. This has appeared in some narrations, but the context of the verse suggests that the removal was out of respect for the station.

In *al-Majma'*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِذِكْرِي** (maintain the prayer for My remembrance) — it was said: its meaning is "maintain the prayer whenever you remember that a prayer is due upon you," whether you were in its time or not. This is from most of the exegetes, and it is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him). It is supported by what Anas narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) who said: **من نسي صلاة فليصلها إذا ذكرها ـ لا كفارة لها غير ذلك** — "Whoever forgets a prayer, let him pray it when he remembers it; there is no expiation for it other than that." Muslim narrated it in the *Sahih*.

**I say:** The hadith is narrated through other chains, both supported and unsupported, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) through Sunni paths, and from the two Sadiqs (peace be upon them) through Shi'a paths.

In *al-Majma'*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَكادُ أُخْفِيها** (I am about to keep it [the Hour] hidden) — it is narrated from Ibn Abbas: "**أَكادُ أُخْفِيها عن نفسي**" (I am about to keep it hidden from Myself), and it is likewise in the recitation of Ubayy. This was also narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Mardawayh, al-Khatib, and Ibn Asakir narrated from Asma bint Umays, who said: "I saw the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) opposite [Mount] Thabir while he was saying: 'Shine, O Thabir! Shine, O Thabir! O Allah, I ask You for what my brother Musa asked You: that You expand for me my breast, and ease for me my affair, and untie the knot from my tongue that they may understand my speech; and appoint for me a minister from my family, Ali my brother; strengthen my back through him, and associate him in my affair, that we may glorify You much and remember You much; indeed You are ever watchful over us.'"

**I say:** A similar meaning was narrated from al-Salfi from al-Baqir (peace be upon him). It was also narrated in *al-Majma'* from Ibn Abbas from Abu Dharr from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in a similar vein.

The author of *Ruh al-Ma'ani* said, after citing the aforementioned hadith: "It is not hidden that it is necessary here to interpret the 'affair' (الأمر) as the matter of guidance and calling to the truth. It is not permissible to interpret it as prophethood (النبوة), nor is it correct to use it as evidence for the immediate caliphate (خلافة) of Ali—may Allah ennoble his face—after the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) without interval."

He mentioned similar points regarding what is authentic of his (peace be upon him) saying to him when he appointed him as his deputy over his family during the expedition of Tabuk: **أما ترضى أن تكون مني بمنزلة هارون من موسى ـ إلا أنه لا نبي بعدي** — "Are you not pleased to be to me as Harun was to Musa, except that there is no prophet after me?" End quote.

I say: As for using the hadith or the "Hadith of the Position" (حديث المنزلة) as evidence for his (peace be upon him) immediate caliphate, the discussion of that is outside the purpose of this book. We are only discussing the meaning of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying in his prayer for Ali (peace be upon him): **وَأَشْرِكْهُ فِي أَمْرِي** (and associate him in my affair), in accordance with the prayer of Musa (peace be upon him) recounted in the Mighty Book. For this has a connection to what he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) understood from the wording of the verse, and the hadith is authentic, supported by the mutawatir (١) Hadith of the Position.

His (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) meaning of "affair" in his saying: **وَأَشْرِكْهُ فِي أَمْرِي** is certainly not prophethood, due to the text of the Hadith of the Position which explicitly excludes prophethood. This is the definitive proof that Musa's meaning of "affair" in his saying: **وَأَشْرِكْهُ فِي أَمْرِي** was not prophethood; otherwise, the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying "my affair" would remain without a beneficial meaning.

Nor is the "affair" meant as absolute guidance and calling to the truth—as he mentioned—certainly not, because that is an obligation (تكليف) that the entire nation carries out, and others share it with him. The proof of the Book and the Sunnah is established in this regard, such as the saying of the Exalted: **قُلْ هذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُوا إِلَى اللهِ عَلى بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي** — **Say, ‘This is my way: I summon to Allah with insight—I and he who follows me’** (Yusuf: 108), and the saying of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—narrated by both the general and the specific [Sunni and Shi'a]: **فليبلغ الشاهد الغائب** — "Let the one who is present convey to the one who is absent." If it were an affair shared among all, there would be no meaning in asking for Ali's association in it.

Furthermore, the possessive construction (الإضافة) in his saying "my affair" (أَمْرِي) implies exclusivity (الاختصاص); thus, it does not apply to what is shared among all. A similar argument applies to the saying of Musa recounted in the verse.

Yes, the initial conveyance (التبليغ الابتدائي) — *which is the conveyance of revelation for the first time* — is an affair exclusive to the Prophet; he does not have the right to delegate another man to convey the original revelation. Thus, association in it is association in his affair. In the saying of Musa there is that which bears witness to this, as he says: **وَأَخِي هارُونُ هُوَ أَفْصَحُ مِنِّي لِساناً فَأَرْسِلْهُ مَعِي رِدْءاً يُصَدِّقُنِي** — **And my brother Harun, he is more eloquent than me in speech. So send him with me as a helper to confirm me** (Al-Qasas: 34). For the meaning of "confirming him" is not merely to say "my brother has spoken the truth," but rather to clarify what was obscure in his speech, detail what was summarized, and convey on his behalf some of the revelation that he was charged to convey.

So this type of conveyance, and the effects of prophethood that accompany it—such as the obligation of obedience—are among the things exclusive to the Prophet, and association in them is association in his affair. This meaning is what is intended by "affair" in his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) prayer, and it is also what is intended—with the addition of prophethood—in the prayer of Musa.

What pertains to this discussion has already been presented in the commentary on the beginning of Surah al-Bara'ah, regarding the hadith of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sending Ali with the first verses of Bara'ah to Mecca after removing Abu Bakr from it, based on what was revealed to him: **أنه لا يبلغها عنك إلا أنت أو رجل منك** — "None shall convey it on your behalf except you or a man from you," in the ninth volume of this book.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: My father narrated to me from al-Hasan ibn Mahbub from al-Ala ibn Razin from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "When his mother conceived him, her pregnancy did not appear until she gave birth to him. Pharaoh had appointed Coptic women over the women of the Children of Israel to watch them. This was because of what had reached him from the Children of Israel—that they were saying: 'A man will be born among us called Musa ibn Imran, and the destruction of Pharaoh and his companions will be at his hands.' Pharaoh said at that: 'I shall surely kill their male children so that what they desire does not happen.' He separated the men and the women and imprisoned the men in jails.

When the mother of Musa gave birth to Musa, she looked at him and grieved for him; she became distressed and wept, saying: 'He will be slaughtered this moment.' Then Allah made the woman appointed over her feel compassion for him, and she said to the mother of Musa: 'What is the matter with you that your color has turned yellow?' She replied: 'I fear that my son will be slaughtered.' She said: 'Do not fear.' No one saw Musa except that they loved him—and that is the saying of Allah: **وَأَلْقَيْتُ عَلَيْكَ مَحَبَّةً مِنِّي** (And I cast upon you love from Me) — so the Coptic woman appointed over her loved him."

In *al-Ilal*, with its chain to Ibn Abi Umayr, who said: I said to Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him): "Inform me about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, to Musa: **اذْهَبا إِلى فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ طَغى فَقُولا لَهُ قَوْلاً لَيِّناً لَعَلَّهُ يَتَذَكَّرُ أَوْ يَخْشى** (Go, both of you, to Pharaoh, for he has indeed rebelled. Speak to him in a soft manner; maybe he will take admonition or fear [Allah])." He said: "As for His saying: **فَقُولا لَهُ قَوْلاً لَيِّناً** (Speak to him in a soft manner), it means address him by his kunya (كنياه) and say to him: 'O Abu Mus'ab.' Pharaoh's kunya was Abu Mus'ab al-Walid ibn Mus'ab. As for His saying: **لَعَلَّهُ يَتَذَكَّرُ أَوْ يَخْشى** (maybe he will take admonition or fear [Allah]), He only said it so that Musa would be more eager to go. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, already knew that Pharaoh would not take admonition nor fear except upon seeing the punishment. Do you not hear Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, saying: **حَتَّى إِذا أَدْرَكَهُ الْغَرَقُ قالَ آمَنْتُ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا الَّذِي آمَنَتْ بِهِ بَنُوا إِسْرائِيلَ وَأَنَا مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ** — **until when the drowning overtook him, he said, ‘I believe that there is no god except Him in whom the Children of Israel believe, and I am one of those who submit’** (Yunus: 90)? But Allah did not accept his faith and said: **آلْآنَ وَقَدْ عَصَيْتَ قَبْلُ وَكُنْتَ مِنَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ** — **[What!] Now? When you were disobedient before and were among the agents of corruption?** (Yunus: 91)."

**I say:** The beginning of the hadith was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Abi Hatim from Ali. The interpretation of "soft speech" as addressing by kunya is of the nature of mentioning some instances (مصاديق) out of necessity, as it is not restricted to that.

The end of the hadith was also narrated in *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Adi ibn Hatim from Ali (peace be upon him). In it is support for what we previously stated: that "maybe" (لعل) is used in the verse for hope (الترجي).
